Q1: I usually organized my study routine around listening to lectures and audio materials since that's how I learn best. I'd set aside about two hours most days, often in the evenings when my house was quieter. I tried to keep a consistent schedule, but honestly with limited resources at home, I had to be creative about when and where I could focus. I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other tasks, which helped me maximize my time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was due. I tried to maintain some regularity, like logging in every other day, but when assignments were coming up, I'd increase my study hours significantly. The stress of deadlines pushed me to be more intense in those periods. I found that having a loose routine helped, but life circumstances meant I couldn't always stick to it perfectly.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was new content posted or when I needed to check on assignment details. Sometimes my peers would message me about discussions happening, and that would prompt me to log in too. I also made it a habit to check in the mornings to see if there were any announcements. The peer support group I was part of really helped keep me accountable for staying connected.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic for me. It happened because I connected with some classmates through the discussion forums, and we'd check in with each other regularly. That social element made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like a place where I had people counting on me. The peer support intervention really helped cement this habit.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the information auditorily. The discussion forums were also valuable because interacting with my peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I found that hearing different perspectives in the discussions actually deepened my understanding more than just reading alone would have.
Q6: I often minimized my use of the reading-heavy PDFs and text resources because they're harder for me to engage with as an auditory learner. I'd skim them but not deeply engage unless absolutely necessary for an assignment. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skip those to save time given my limited study hours.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when it directly connected to something I needed to understand for assignments or exams, or when it involved interaction with others. If I could hear someone explaining a concept or discuss it with peers, I felt like I was actually learning. Activities that felt isolated or purely text-based didn't engage me as much.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given my time constraints and limited resources. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so I prioritized those. But sometimes the optional discussion threads were where the best learning happened because people were more relaxed and open. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because a peer recommended it.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download audio files and lectures to my phone and listen to them during commutes or while doing chores at home. I also took handwritten notes while listening, which helped me process information better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen where the LMS couldn't track it.
Q10: There were many times I'd listen to a downloaded lecture two or three times over several days, but the LMS would only show one view. I'd also have long conversations with my study group over WhatsApp where we'd work through problems together. Those discussions were some of my most productive learning moments, but they happened completely outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access a video or download materials once, then study them offline for days. With my internet access being somewhat limited at times, it made more sense to grab what I needed and work with it locally. The LMS would show minimal activity during my most intensive study periods because of this.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my stress levels peaked and engagement became harder. Around midterms, I felt overwhelmed by the workload across all my courses. My low resilience meant I struggled to bounce back quickly from setbacks. During those times, the peer support really helped pull me through because my classmates would check in on me.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me, especially since I was managing a lot with limited resources at home. When I was exhausted, I'd still log into the LMS but wouldn't engage as deeply. Competing responsibilities like family obligations sometimes meant I had to rush through materials rather than absorb them properly. The high stress definitely affected the quality of my engagement.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere because I was stressed or tired. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Those moments felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' on the platform. I learned to recognize when this was happening and would try to take a break instead.
Q15: I sometimes postponed the longer reading assignments because they felt daunting and didn't match my learning style. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later. The anxiety of potentially not understanding the text made me avoid it, which wasn't helpful. I eventually learned to pair readings with peer discussions to make them more manageable.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd log in more frequently, watch more videos, and participate more in discussions when deadlines approached. It wasn't always the healthiest pattern, but the urgency helped me focus despite my stress levels.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I received positive feedback on assignments, it boosted my motivation to engage more with similar content. Constructive criticism made me revisit materials and discussions to improve. The peer feedback in discussions was especially impactful because it felt more immediate and personal than instructor comments.
Q18: The course structure with regular video releases worked well for my auditory learning style. Having weekly modules gave me a rhythm to follow even when things got chaotic. The built-in discussion requirements pushed me to interact with peers, which ended up being one of the most valuable parts. The structure provided scaffolding that helped manage my stress.
Q19: The discussion forums being integrated into the course made engagement easier because I could learn from peers while completing requirements. However, the heavy reliance on text-based readings made some aspects harder for me. Having more audio options or recorded explanations of readings would have helped. The peer support intervention was a design element that significantly improved my experience.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it, so they keep clicking around. Or they could be dealing with stress or anxiety that makes it hard to actually complete work even though they're trying. Sometimes high activity reflects struggle rather than success. It could also be that their learning is happening in ways that don't translate to submitted assignments.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam scores and grades didn't always match my LMS activity because so much of my real learning happened offline. When I listened to lectures multiple times on my phone or discussed concepts with peers outside the platform, that productive work wasn't captured. Low activity doesn't mean low engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of when students are active and which resources they click on. They can track completion rates and time spent on the platform. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, peer conversations happening elsewhere, and the emotional state of the student. They can't capture whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and wanting to understand more. It's when I find myself discussing concepts with peers even outside of class requirements. For me, engagement is feeling like I'm part of a learning community, not just consuming content alone. It's both intellectual involvement and emotional investment in my progress.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support intervention changed my experience and kept me motivated during high-stress periods. It wouldn't capture the surprise and gratitude I felt when classmates reached out to help me. My offline listening sessions, my handwritten notes, my WhatsApp study group conversations—none of that appears in the data. The human connections that made this course meaningful are invisible to the system.
